section_name: What is a Family Firm and Why Does It Matter? A Survey of Family Firm Definitions 1
text: A Survey of Family Firm Definitions 1

section_name: What is a Family Firm and Why Does It Matter? A Survey of Family Firm Definitions 1
text: Morten Bennedsen, University of Copenhagen and INSEAD Yi-Chun Lu Soochow University Vikas Mehrotra University of Alberta

section_name: Abstract
text: We present a systematic survey of how family firms are defined in empirical studies, focusing on five key conceptual dimensions: ownership, control, management, embeddedness, and succession. Our findings highlight three main points: First, the definition of family firms significantly influences key outcomes, such as their prevalence, performance relative to nonfamily firms, and innovation activity. Second, among our sample of 122 papers, 29 different binary definitions are used. Third, the most common criteria for defining family firms are ownership, control, and management participation. We conclude by recommending that future research in family business prioritize robustness analyses using multiple definitions of family firms.

section_name: Abstract
text: 1 We thank JiYoung Kim, Aremis Chung, Maithili Dipak Modi, Jihye Jang, Ziyou Zhang, Mengqi Li and in particular Roveena Mecwan for excellent research assistance. We are grateful for financial funding from the Danish National Research Foundation (Niels Bohr Professorship) and the Danish Finance Institute.

section_name: Introduction
text: How to distinguish the theoretical and operational definitions of family firms was proposed

section_name: Introduction
text: by Chua, Chrisman, and Sharma (1999)(CCS, 1999, henceforth) in their seminal work,

section_name: Introduction
text: indicating that the former “... must capture the essence differentiating family businesses from all

section_name: Introduction
text: other firms ” (CCS, 1999: 24) and represents the “... the standard against which operational definitions must be measured .” 2 (CCS, 1999: 19). This question reflects the core issue in any field of research: the requirement of precise definitions of research objects. As noted in Bird,

section_name: Introduction
text: Welsch, Astrachan and Pistrui (2002), family business research should be found on conceptual

section_name: Introduction
text: definitions, and these should drive operational definitions into empirical research. 3 In this paper

section_name: Introduction
text: we document the importance of definitions in studying family firms, a paucity of conceptually

section_name: Introduction
text: driven operational definitions, and a general lack of convergence among operational definitions.

section_name: Introduction
text: As noted by Miller, Le Breton-Miller, Lester, & Cannella (2007), a lack of definitional

section_name: Introduction
text: consensus has implications for the validity and robustness of inferences one can draw from

section_name: Introduction
text: studying family firm. For instance, Handler (1989) notes that “ Without having clear criteria for

section_name: Introduction
text: distinguishing family firms from other types of business, research sampling is very difficult. ” We

section_name: Introduction
text: document how empirical research results depend crucially on the operational definition within

section_name: Introduction
text: fundamental queries such as the prevalence of family firm, their performance relative to non-

section_name: Introduction
text: family firms and their innovation activity.

section_name: Introduction
text: 2 Quoted from the abstract. 3 Bird, Welsch, Astrachan and Pistrui (2002) conclude by noting key deficiencies in the field of family business research and admonish that “... the definition of family business needs to be articulated clearly. ”

section_name: Introduction
text: 3 Conceptually the family firm definition is multi-dimensional and family firms represent a

section_name: Introduction
text: heterogeneous group along the various dimensions that define them. 4 We illustrate the multi-

section_name: Introduction
text: dimensionality through the well-known French luxury brand, Hermès. It is today a publicly

section_name: Introduction
text: traded company where more than 70 family members in three family branches own

section_name: Introduction
text: approximately 70 percent of the firm’s shares; family control is further secured through family

section_name: Introduction
text: trust. Its current CEO is Axel Dumas, a 6 th generation family scion with a direct blood line to the

section_name: Introduction
text: founder. Two-thirds of non-employee members of the board are family members or closely

section_name: Introduction
text: related, and young family members are actively encouraged to get involved in the firm. Hermès

section_name: Introduction
text: illustrates the multi-dimensional aspect of being a family firm – these are direct ownership, a

section_name: Introduction
text: control securing trust, board membership, senior management positions, family embeddedness in

section_name: Introduction
text: the business and intra-family succession. (Bennedsen, Crawford, Hoefer, 2014).

section_name: Introduction
text: This survey makes three contributions. First, despite the large and increasing research

section_name: Introduction
text: volume in studying family firms, the lack of consensus in the definition of family firms

section_name: Introduction
text: contributes to a bias in studying the difference between family firms and their non-family

section_name: Introduction
text: counterparts. This lack of consensus will also lead to a tough task in generalizing the findings

section_name: Introduction
text: from studying family firms. We sort through prior empirical research and identify five key

section_name: Introduction
text: dimensions in defining family firms about which there is widespread agreement in the literature

section_name: Introduction
text: of entrepreneurship, family business research, management, economics, and finance. Second, we

section_name: Introduction
text: highlight the fact that most operational definitions often narrowly focus on one or two

section_name: Introduction
text: dimensions which are selected due to data availability rather than driven by conceptual analysis.

section_name: Introduction
text: 4 Amore, Bennedsen, Le Breton‐Miller, & Miller (2021) posit that the heterogeneity of family firms, along with a tendency for family firms to deliver extreme performance, has led to a plethora of evidence documenting both their superiority and their inferiority vis-à-vis non-family firms.

section_name: Introduction
text: 4 While in principle operational definitions may vary across research fields and research questions,

section_name: Introduction
text: in practice we find that variations in operational family firm definitions persist within research

section_name: Introduction
text: fields and in papers that address similar research questions.

section_name: Introduction
text: Our third contribution is to identify the embeddedness of the family in business and

section_name: Introduction
text: succession intent and practice across generations 5 are surprisingly rarely adopted as part of

section_name: Introduction
text: operational definitions, while the other three dimensions of ownership, management, and control

section_name: Introduction
text: are employed with a wide latitude, albeit seldom in the same study. For example, ownership is

section_name: Introduction
text: used as a sole or sufficient criterion to qualify as a family firm in 47% of the definitions in our

section_name: Introduction
text: survey, while family management is a sole or sufficient criterion in only 15% of the studies.

section_name: Introduction
text: Within ownership-based definitions, there is a wide latitude in specific thresholds, ranging from

section_name: Introduction
text: 1 share to more than 50% of shares, with 10% ownership being a common threshold. Succession

section_name: Introduction
text: intention, either by itself or in combination with ownership or management criteria, features in

section_name: Introduction
text: only 8% of the surveyed papers as a necessary criterion for qualifying as a family firm. Being

section_name: Introduction
text: embedded in the business features in 14% of the definitions as a sole criterion or in combination

section_name: Introduction
text: with other dimensions.

section_name: Introduction
text: The absence of embeddedness and succession in most studies can lead to biased inferences

section_name: Introduction
text: regarding their prevalence and performance. As an example, consider the US company Microsoft

section_name: Introduction
text: founded by Bill Gates. Until recently, he was the largest individual shareholder in the company,

section_name: Introduction
text: and throughout the 1990s served as its CEO. He currently has no executive or board positions at

section_name: Introduction
text: 5 Several earlier studies have conceptually discussed one or more of these dimensions. Control and ownership is the focus of Handler, 1989, and Anderson and Reeb, 2003a. Succession and family embeddedness into the business is key to Donnelley, 1964; and Churchill and Hatten, 1987. Succession as an integral part of family firms also appears in Chua et al., (1999) and Garcia, Sharma, De Massis, Wright, & Scholes (2018).

section_name: Introduction
text: 5 Microsoft, none of his three children are involved in any capacity in the firm, and there is no plan

section_name: Introduction
text: for succession, nor was there ever one, within the Gates family. All studies 6 that use founder

section_name: Introduction
text: ownership thresholds as a sufficient criterion for defining family firms would classify Microsoft

section_name: Introduction
text: as one during Bill Gates’ tenure as CEO so long as Gates’ ownership exceeds the selected

section_name: Introduction
text: threshold. We submit that Microsoft, during Mr. Gates’ tenure, was a founder-controlled firm,

section_name: Introduction
text: like an entrepreneur firm, and not a family firm. Similarly, in studies of private firms where the

section_name: Introduction
text: number of owners is limited, almost all firms risk being categorized as family firms, regardless

section_name: Introduction
text: of succession intentions. 7

section_name: Introduction
text: A second type of mis-categorization is illustrated by the Japanese company Suzuki. Ever

section_name: Introduction
text: since it became public in 1949, the founding Suzuki family has never been listed among the top

section_name: Introduction
text: ten shareholders of their iconic namesake company (Bennedsen, Mehrotra, Wiwattanakantang, &

section_name: Introduction
text: Henry, 2022). For more than 70 years average family ownership has been less than one percent.

section_name: Introduction
text: Suzuki’s largest shareholders are long term institutional investors, both domestic and foreign.

section_name: Introduction
text: Nevertheless, the Suzuki family has controlled their namesake company without having a

section_name: Introduction
text: material ownership stake. Osamu Suzuki, until very recently the CEO of the firm, entered the

section_name: Introduction
text: Suzuki family through an arranged marriage to the eldest daughter of Suzuki’s 2 nd CEO, in fact,

section_name: Introduction
text: all of Suzuki’s CEOs, save the current one, have been adopted sons and married to daughters in

section_name: Introduction
text: 6 Studies that include ownership by a single person or family above a certain threshold (typically 5-25 percent for public traded firms) as a necessary and sufficient condition include, among other, Anderson, Reeb and Zhao (2012), Ang, Cole and Lin (2000), Bertrand, Johnson, Samphantharak, & Schoar (2008), Bunkanwanicha, Fan & Wiwattanakantang (2013), Chirico, Criago, Bau, Naldi, Gomez-Maija & Kotlar (2020), Faccio and Lang (2002), Franks, Mayer, Volpin, & Wagner (2009; 2012), Gomez-Mejia (2007), Keasey, Martinez & Pintado (2015), Kowalewski, Talavera, & Stetsyuk (2010), Miller and Breton-Miller (2006), Mullins and Schoar (2016), PérezGonzáles (2006), Sraer and Thesmar (2007). 7 Notable exceptions include Carney & Child (2013) where the authors employ multiple definitions of private family firms, documenting a tremendous heterogeneity in private family firms depending on the precise definition used to classify them.

section_name: Introduction
text: 6 the blood line of the founder. Suzuki is an example of an important group of dynastic controlled

section_name: Introduction
text: family firms where the family exercises control with a miniscule ownership stake (Bennedsen,

section_name: Introduction
text: Mehrotra, Shim, & Wiwattanakantang, 2021). Studies that rely on minimum ownership

section_name: Introduction
text: thresholds by the founding family, say one percent or higher, would categorize dynastic

section_name: Introduction
text: controlled firms such as Suzuki as non-family firms. 8

section_name: Introduction
text: Our findings echoed previous empirical papers with multi-dimensional definitions.

section_name: Introduction
text: Donnelley (1964) - one of the earliest contributions to the family business research - defines a

section_name: Introduction
text: family firm as “ A company is considered a family firm when it has been closely identified with at

section_name: Introduction
text: least two generations of a family and when this link has had a mutual influence on company

section_name: Introduction
text: policy and on the interests and objectives of the family .” (Donnelley, 1964: 428). According to

section_name: Introduction
text: Donnelley’s definition, of the three examples above, Hermès and Suzuki qualify as family firms,

section_name: Introduction
text: but Microsoft does not. If instead we went by a definition of family firms based on founding

section_name: Introduction
text: family ownership, Hermès and Microsoft in the Bill Gates area would be counted as family

section_name: Introduction
text: firms, but Suzuki and other dynastic controlled firms would not. 9 The potential for overand

section_name: Introduction
text: under-counting the prevalence of family firms in an economy is substantial. 10

section_name: Introduction
text: 8 Studies that have ownership above a certain threshold level as a necessary condition for being a family firms include all papers mention in footnote 2 and papers such as Bandiera, Lemos, Prat & Sadun (2018), Bertrand and Schoar, 2006, Claessens, Djankov, & Lang (2000), De Cesari, Gonenc, & Ozkan (2016), Ellul, Pagano & Schvardi (2018), Franks, Mayer & Mijajima (2014), Gomez-Mejia, Larraza-Kintana, & Makri, (2003), Le Breton-Miller, Miller, & Lester (2011), Masulis, Pham & Zein (2011), Miller, Amore, Le Breton-Miller, Minichilli, & Quarato (2018) and Sirmon, Arregle, Hitt & Webb (2008). 9 A similar point regarding the essentialism of succession in the definition of family firms is made in Churchill and Hatten (1987). 10 For example, Bennedsen, Mehrotra, Shim, & Wiwattanakantang (2021) estimates that more than 7 pct of public traded firm in Japan are dynastic controlled firms where families control firms with less than 5 pct of the shares and thus are mis-categorized in the family business literature.

section_name: Introduction
text: 7 The outline of this paper is as follows. Section 2 provides an overview of family firms’

section_name: Introduction
text: definition from previous studies and Section 3 illustrates the method of the survey. Section we

section_name: Introduction
text: report the synthesis of the findings from the survey and include the paper with the discussion in

section_name: 1. The importance of family firm definition in estimating the frequency, the
text: 13 Chrisman, Chua, De Massis, & Wright (2015) reported that family firms have superior ability yet lower willingness to engage in technological innovation. De Massis, Di Minin, & Frattini, 2015). De Massis, Frattini, Kotlar, Petruzzelli, & Wright (2016) conceptualize a new product innovation strategyinnovation through tradition (ITT) to identify the way family firms use their past as the source for future innovation. Furthermore, antecedents from family system are also examined to unveil the innovation decisions in family firm. Rondi, De Massis, & Kotlar (2019) developed a typology of four ideal types of Seasoner , Re-enactor , Digger , and Adventurer with how family firms position themselves as making innovative decisions. Filser, De Massis, Gast, Kraus, & Niemand (2018) investigated family SMEs and reported that intrafamily succession will renew family bonds and positively affect innovativeness, while the identification of family members with the firm provides a negative effect on innovativeness.

text: 2.3 Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of a family firm

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: In this section we discuss conceptually the multi-dimensional aspects of defining family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: firms and the challenges of imposing a complex definition in empirical research. It is clear from

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the discussion above that the label family firms have been used on a large and heterogeneous

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: group of firms that may share a common element of overlap between a family and a business but

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: also can be diverse in many important aspects. It is tempting to abolish the idea of a binary

text: categorization of family firms and non-family firms, given the heterogeneity that will always

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: exist within the groups. However, a clear majority of published articles have used a binary

text: definition 14 which makes it important to understand the underlying elements in defining family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Few notable studies incorporated multi-dimensions in defining family firms. Inspired by

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Donnelly (1964), we incorporated both the interaction between the business and the family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Notwithstanding the twin criteria of embeddedness and intra-family succession, the extant

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the

However, even here, the resulting definition is binary – the sliding scale is used as a robustness check in how strictly board control is defined.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 12 literature has not converged on a consensus for the definition of family firms. One reason for the

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: lack of consensus is that there are many elements involved in defining a family’s involvement in

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the business. Below, we highlight five non-exclusive elements of family firm definition, and

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: these elements will constitute the foundation for our empirical survey in the next sections.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Ownership (O): Does a family have controlling ownership? Ownership is a key element for families being able to control their family firm. The three

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: branches of the Hermès family own around 70 percent of the shares in the public listed luxury

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: company. In addition, they have implemented a family trust to protect the family control of the

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: shares in case family members want to exit ownership. In long living family firms, families keep

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: almost all shares within the family through centuries: the world’s oldest family firm, the Hoshi

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Ryokan in Japan, has been owned by one family over 46 generations going back to 818

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: (Bennedsen, Itoh, Stabile, & Henry, 2023).

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Ownership in literature is typically defined as a fraction of shares or cash flow rights. This

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: can vary significantly across types of family firms and countries. This variation is partly linked

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: to the ability to control a firm. Since private firms tend to have few owners, it is common to use a

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 50 percent threshold level in the definition of family firms. Public-traded firms often have

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: hundreds or thousands of owners and thus control of a company can be secured by a smaller

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: ownership stake. In the USA control can be obtained by as little as 5 percent of the shares, in

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Europe threshold levels would typically be higher and in Asia families typically own an even

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: larger share of their public traded firms. In addition to the control derived from the share of

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: ownership, control can also be obtained through control enhancing ownership devices such as

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 13 pyramidal ownership structures, share classes with different voting rights, specific clauses in the

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: corporate charter etc. Control enhancing mechanisms allow families to control their firms with a

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: lower share of nominal ownership.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Management (M): Is the CEO or board Chair a member of the founding family? As we saw above having a family CEO at the helm of the family firm is important for

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: corporate policy, strategic decisions, and the consequences in business (e.g., Gallucci, Santulli,

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: & Calabrò, 2015; Casillas, Moreno-Menéndez, Barbero, & Clinton, 2019). The family heir

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Benjamin William Keswick is the group CEO of Jardine Matheson, the Hong Kong based

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: trading conglomerate. Having a family member at the top becomes a flag that unites the 400,000

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: employees across the world. Similarly, when Axel Dumas Hermès replaced Patrick Thomas at

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the helm of the French luxury company it was explicitly motivated by the importance of having

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the family name in the family company.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: The requirement of a family CEO is often introduced in research studies to exclude two

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: types of firms. The first are firms in which a family or a person is a strategic investor without

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: being involved in the daily operation of the business. The second is where the family keeps being

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: a significant owner but has lost their attachment to the firm and represents passive investors

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: without any engagement in the firm’s activities.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Board (B): Is a member of the founding family represented on the firm’s board of directors? For large family firms, board control is often the most important channel through which

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: families control their business. La Roche is a family firm with large family ownership by the two

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 14 founding families Hoffmann and La Roche. The management is professional and there are no

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: family members in the executive suite. However, the family engages in the overall long-term

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: firm strategies through Andrè Hoffmann and Jörg Duschmalè who represent the two founding

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: families on the firm’s the board of directors. This is a common case in large family firms, where

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the family professionalizes the management and engages in strategy through board

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Embeddedness (E): How engaged is the founding family in the firm, and how dependent are the firm’s policies on the founding family’s preferences, networks and intangible assets? Embeddedness focuses on the degree to which the family and the business are integrated

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: and grow together in a mutually dependent manner (Aldrich & Cliff, 2003). How engaged are

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: family members in the business? How important are the intangible family assets – such as

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: network, legacy and family values – for the success of the family business? Embeddedness

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: constructs a context where there is dyadic interrelation between a kinship-oriented social family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: system and a profit-driven institution15 and is therefore a key element of being a family firm

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: (Sharma , 2004; Aldrich & Cliff, 2003; Chrisman, Chua, & Steier, 2003; Miller, Breton-Miller,

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: & Lester ,2011) adopt social embeddedness (Granovetter, 1985) to see family firm as a business

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: within a social group, and the involvement of family members as owner and manager leads to

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: distinct conduct and performance with agent or stewardship motivation.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 15 The exact definition of embeddedness varies across papers: Baines and Wheelock (1998) introduce the term “family economic unit” to describe family firms (Cruz, Justo, & De Castro, 2012, 63) whereas Khanin, Turel, & Mahto (2012) defined family-business embeddedness as “confluence of values and objectives stemming from various institutional contexts—family, business, and symbolic—within the family firm”.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 15 Borrowing from Donnelley, we submit that a family firm has idiosyncratic ties with the founding family. Thus, embeddedness implies that a link between family preferences and firm policy and in such firm level decisions as employment, payouts, capital structure, and risk

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: management. Put simply, without the embeddedness imperative, the family’s control is no

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: different from that of a non-family owner or a professional manager. 16 , 17

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Succession (S). Has there been a family succession in the firm or is it the manifest intention of the founder to make a family succession? Most firms start out as entrepreneurial firms. As an illustration, a serial entrepreneur sets up

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: a software company with the aim of existing in 10 years. The founder is the sole owner, the CEO

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: and the chairman of the board. The founder places family members on board because they

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: represent available resources and provide trust in governance. After 15 years the entrepreneur

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: sells to an industry leader and plays no role in the future of the company hereafter. During these

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 15 years, the firm would be defined as a family firm by all of the above 4 criteria but it is an

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: entrepreneurial start up firm. Adding the criterion of actual or intentional succession from the

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: pool of family heirs separates the universe of entrepreneur firms into those that are intended to be

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: bequeathed to heirs vs. those where such intentions were unlikely to have been harbored. Family 16 For e.g., Sraer and Thesmar (2007) note that family firms make a “... more parsimonious use of capital ”, indicating lower leverage, less specialized labor, higher NPV acquisitions and a lower cost of debt. Burkart, Panunzi, &Shleifer (2003) note three aspects of family firms that resemble the embeddedness criterion in Donnelley, viz. amenity control, legacy, and the possibility of tunneling. In each case, the firm’s investment and financing policies are co-determined with its family control. 17 Family embeddedness perspective argues that every entrepreneur comes from a family, which is the basic social institution in human society. Granovetter (1985; 1992a, b) analyzed and conceptualized embeddedness in modern societies, specifying the context that economic actions and outcome of these "actors” in modern societies are not merely economic driven; instead, these actions and outcomes are affected both by actors’ “dyadic relationship and the broad structure of their overall network of relationships.” (Hagedoorn, 2006, 671). With this premise Aldrich & Cliff (2003) argued that family members and family resource play a vital role in opportunity cognition and new venture creation.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 16 business scholars have longed to focus on succession as the key element of being a family firm 18

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: and a key challenge for the continuity of the firm (Bennedsen, Fan, Jian, & Yeh 2015; Chrisman

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: & Sharma, 2003; Lansberg, 1988; Sharma, Christman, & Chua, 1996)

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Some economists, including Schumpeter (1934), intuiting an entrepreneur’s motivations,

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: have ascribed a dynastic motive to entrepreneurs, among other motives. It is possible, but by no

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: means a certainty, that all entrepreneurs intend to bequeath their firm to their blood heirs. We

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: propose a simple test to gauge such intentions, per Donnelley’s definition – the presence and

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: involvement of the second generation as a condition in bestowing family firm status. Those

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: entrepreneurs who are moved by dynastic motives would be succeeded by kin; those that were

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: moved by other motivations would be succeeded by non-kin. We classify firms in the former

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: group as family firms, and firms in the latter group as non-family firms. This classification is

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: based on revealed preference for family succession. 19

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 3.2 Empirical challenges in implementing the 5 dimensions

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Empirical research on family firms based on a large dataset requires that definitions are

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: quantified in a consistent manner. This is challenging for several reasons: First, it requires

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: aggregation of the multiple dimensions described above. Thus, the researcher needs to rank the 18 Handler (1994) points out that succession is “ the most important issue that most family firms face . “(Handler, 1994, p1) and Ward highlights the distinctiveness of family firm in terms of its potential to the existence in generational concern. “ We define a family business as one that will be passed on for the family's next generation to manage and control " (Ward, 1987, p252). Previous work focused less on intentions and more on succession planning (e.g., Christensen, 1953; Tashakori, 1977; Rosenblatt, de Mik, Anderson, & Johnson, 1985), Dyer (1986) and Handler (1987 and 1989). 19 We recognize that in some cases the revealed preference may be at odds with the initial intent of the founder. We believe this noise in the ex-post classification is unlikely to introduce bias because the intent and revealed preference could go either way. That is, a founder may have intentions of bequeathing the firm to his progeny, but circumstance might transpire against that intention and the firm might get classified as a non-family firm in due time. Or a founder may have little intent of passing his firm to his children, but after a sudden death, the progeny decides to take over.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 17 different elements above and construct an aggregate binary variable as a weighted average of the

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: underlying elements. 20 Second, many dimensions are not binary. The Kirk-Kristensen family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: owns 100 percent of the parent company of the famous toy company LEGO, but the Siemens

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: family owns only 7 to 8 percent of their famous namesake company. On top of this, family

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: shares may have superior voting rights or there may be rules saying that family shares can only

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: be sold within the family. Thus, ownership is a continuum, and control is often a non-linear

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: function of ownership. As we discussed above many studies take a short cut and define a binary

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: variable of ownership and control based on thresholds that can vary from 5 to 50 percent.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Third, the ability to observe ownership is limited by national corporate laws and access to

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: data. Corporate laws often do not require disclosure of detailed individual ownership stakes.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Publicly traded firms often require disclosure of the identity of key shareholders with ownership

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: stakes above certain threshold levels, but exact shareholdings disclosure is not mandated. For

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: private firms, the requirement is often weaker. Thus, the researcher relies on either access to

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: commercial ownership databases or must collect information from the company or other

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: websites. In both cases, exact ownership statistics may be difficult to establish.

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Fourth, the ability to observe the family’s involvement in management and labor varies

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: across countries and across data registers. However, since management position and board

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: positions are binary variables, this is in general less of a challenge than obtaining detailed

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: ownership information. Fifth, whereas the embeddedness criterion is a powerful conceptual

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 20 Given the multidimensional definition of family ownership, involvement and control, a natural approach is to start with a plenary group of all firms and then analyze how the family structure impacts governance (see, e.g., Bennedsen, Nielsen, Perez-Gonzalez, & Wolfenzon, 2007; Bertrand & Schoar, 2006). This approach may lead to an endogenous definition where the degree of family involvement along the dimensions noted above determines the degree of family firm, in a continuous manner. As it is, most papers in the literature take a binary approach and categorize firms into either a family or a non-family firm

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: 18 dimension of what it means to be a family firm, it is difficult to implement the same in empirical

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: studies. For instance, there are many ways to define the overlap between family and business and

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: the amount of measurable data available is often very limited. Not surprisingly, very few studies

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: use this criterion in a systematic manner, and when they do it is often based on the number of

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: family members working or directly engaged in the firm (see for example Bennedsen,

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Tsoutsoura & Wolfenzon, 2022).

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: Finally, whereas information about actual succession is possible to collect, it is challenging

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: to define ex-ante intent in family successions. First generation firms, where the founder harbors a

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: strong interest in intra-family succession, may receive a tempting offer to sell. For the researcher,

section_name: 2. Five dimensions in the conceptual definition of family firms and the
text: it is difficult to separate such cases from a strictly entrepreneurial case where exit was the goal

section_name: 3. A survey
text: In this section we provide the method and the results of our survey of how empirical papers

section_name: 3. A survey
text: have defined family firms. The survey followed a systematic process to identify and review

section_name: 3. A survey
text: relevant literature. We began by making structured surveys on different search engines, including

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Google Scholar, Web of Science, and JSTOR with the keywords of "define family business,"

section_name: 3. A survey
text: "define family firm," "family ownership," and "family-controlled firms". We limited the search

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 21 The accompanying Internet Appendix provide a table of all studies included in the survey. For each paper it provides data on how a family firm is defined and how this definition relates to the 5 categories above.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 19 to publications between 1964 and 2022. The 1964 starting point was chosen because of the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: seminal paper of Donelly that was published in that year.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Next, we restrict journals with impact factors greater than 3 22 . The selected articles

section_name: 3. A survey
text: underwent a detailed review by the three authors to ensure alignment with the study’s objectives.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: The review process emphasized two criteria: a clear definition of "family business" or "family

section_name: 3. A survey
text: firm" and the inclusion of and empirical analysis containing a sample of family firms. Our

section_name: 3. A survey
text: process ended in selecting articles from 27 journals across the domains of businessfinance,

section_name: 3. A survey
text: economics, and management. With the multidisciplinary journal pools, it allows us to examine

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the differences in defining family firms across different research fields. Meanwhile, FT 50 list is

section_name: 3. A survey
text: also used to identify the overlap of the journal list. There are 16 journals on the FT 50 list with

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 11 papers from Journal of Financial Economics as the highest number of papers compared to the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: peers on the list. In the journals which are not on the FT 50 list, Journal of Corporate Finance is

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the one with highest number of papers, revealing 14 papers in the time frame.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: To identify the papers with operation definitions and empirical evidence, the first three

section_name: 3. A survey
text: pages of search results were reviewed by the three authors to finalize the sample of papers. This

section_name: 3. A survey
text: process resulted in a final sample of 122 papers. The distribution of articles across journals (and

section_name: 3. A survey
text: working papers) is illustrated in Figure 1 .

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Table 1 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 22 Clarivate's 2022 journal list

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 20 We classify these journals into two broad categories. The first category contains journals in

section_name: 3. A survey
text: entrepreneurship, management and strategy that have traditionally had a focus on family

section_name: 3. A survey
text: business studies (such as Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice , Family Business Review , and

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the Journal of Family Business Strategies ). The second group contains journals with a financial

section_name: 3. A survey
text: economics focus such as the Journal of Finance , Journal of Financial Economics , Review of

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Financial studies , and the Journal of Financials and Quantitative Analysis ). For simplicity we

section_name: 3. A survey
text: refer to these two categories as Management and Finance journals in the following analysis.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Almost half of the surveyed papers come from three Finance and three Management journals:

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Family Business Review and Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice are the top two in

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Management journals, whereas the Journal of Corporate Finance and the Journal of Financial

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Economics are the top two in Finance journals.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Figure 1 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Figure 2 shows the distribution of articles according to year of publication. More than 90%

section_name: 3. A survey
text: of studies were published after 2000. This is indicative of the growth in the empirical family

section_name: 3. A survey
text: business literature during the last three decades. As mentioned above, we consider Donnelley’s

section_name: 3. A survey
text: paper (Donnelley, 1964) as the founding paper in empirical family business literature.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Figure 2 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 4.2: How empirical papers define a family firm

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 21 We now turn to a taxonomy of the five criteria identified above and categorize the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: definitions in the surveyed papers on the five criteria: ownership, management, board,

section_name: 3. A survey
text: embeddedness and succession. We analyze every definition in our survey based on each of the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: five dimensions that is used to define a family firm. We examine whether the definition requires

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the different criteria as being necessary, sufficient or simply an alternate criterion for a firm to be

section_name: 3. A survey
text: labelled a family firm.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Figure 3 summarizes the elements of family firm definitions across the articles. Since some

section_name: 3. A survey
text: articles use several definitions, we end up with 149 definitions from 122 papers. 23 The figure

section_name: 3. A survey
text: provides two important insights. First, there is a large variation in the definition of family firm

section_name: 3. A survey
text: across published articles. We identify 28 different definitions employed in the surveyed papers –

section_name: 3. A survey
text: these definitions differ based on various permutations of the five criteria that are used to define

section_name: 3. A survey
text: family firms. It is worth remarking that even within these categories there exists considerable

section_name: 3. A survey
text: variation with several concerns.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Figure 3 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: First, ownership is the most popular of the five criteria. A sizable majority of the definitions

section_name: 3. A survey
text: use ownership as either a sole and sufficient criterion, or in combination with other criteria to

section_name: 3. A survey
text: define a family firm. We find that 38.3% of definitions are based solely on ownership by the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: founding family. If we count the inclusion of ownership in combination with the other four

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 23 Articles with multiple definitions of family firms include Anderson, Mansi, & Reeb (2003), Andres (2008), Barontini & Caprio (2005), Bennedsen, Fan, Ming & Yin-Hua (2015), Chen, Chen, Cheng, & Shevlin (2010), Duran, Van Essen, Heugens, Kostova, & Peng (2019), Le Breton-Miller, Miller & Lester (2011), Miller, BretonMiller, & Lester (2013), Maury (2006), Masulis, Pham, & Zein (2011), Murro & Peruzzi (2019), Rose & Chung (2012), Steijvers & Niskanen (2014) and Villalonga & Amit (2006). Villalonga & Amit (2006) has indeed 9 different definitions of family firms.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 22 criteria to define a family firm, we find that more than 8 out 10 definitions include ownership as

section_name: 3. A survey
text: a sole or joint criterion to define a family firm. Despite the prevalence of using ownership as a

section_name: 3. A survey
text: sole or a joint criterion, there is little consensus in literature on what this threshold should be.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Some papers use 5%, others as high as 50%, and yet others count any positive level as sufficient.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Second, top management is the second most popular criterion in defining family firms.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 5.4% of studies that accept it as a sole criterion and more than one-fifth of the studies use top

section_name: 3. A survey
text: management as a joint criterion in defining family firms, while we cannot find a clear pattern

section_name: 3. A survey
text: among the use of the other dimensions. Board positions are the next most commonly used

section_name: 3. A survey
text: criteria in family firm definitions. Last but not lease, in considering the intra-family succession

section_name: 3. A survey
text: as a condition to define family firms, only 4.2% of the definitions in our survey explicitly

section_name: 3. A survey
text: acknowledge a requirement of it. Recall that the seminal paper by Donnelley (1964) counts

section_name: 3. A survey
text: succession as an integral part of a family firm, alongside the notion of embeddedness.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Surprisingly, we did not find any studies, except that by Donnelley, in our survey that require the

section_name: 3. A survey
text: twin dimensions of succession and embeddedness to define a family firm. The concept of

section_name: 3. A survey
text: embeddedness is also conspicuous by the relative absence in the criteria commonly used to

section_name: 3. A survey
text: define family firms.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Multiple criteria within one paper: We next turn to examine the use of single vs. multiple

section_name: 3. A survey
text: criteria in defining family firms. We begin by investigating the extent to which the studies in our

section_name: 3. A survey
text: survey employ multiple criteria in defining family firms, sometimes as jointly necessary, and

section_name: 3. A survey
text: sometimes as alternative criteria from a set. The number of dimensions used in the survey is

section_name: 3. A survey
text: illustrated in Figure 4 . It shows that 44% of the definitions employ only one dimension in

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 23 defining family firms, 25% of the definitions in our survey use two criteria, and 21% use three

section_name: 3. A survey
text: criteria to define a family firm. In general, studies published in Finance journals are more likely

section_name: 3. A survey
text: to rely on a single dimension, usually ownership, to define a family firm, while Management

section_name: 3. A survey
text: journals are more likely to employ two or more criteria to define the family firms.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Figure 4 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Necessary and sufficient elements of the definition of a family firm: We investigate to what

section_name: 3. A survey
text: extent certain criteria are strictly necessary to qualify as a family firm, vis-à-vis other dimensions

section_name: 3. A survey
text: that serve as alternates in an either/or requirement. Put simply, we want to know if, say, a board

section_name: 3. A survey
text: position by the family member is a non-negotiable part of the definition of family firms, or if it

section_name: 3. A survey
text: can be substituted with another criterion such as a top management position. In Figure 5 we

section_name: 3. A survey
text: show how often each of the five criteria is used as a strict or weak requirement in the definition

section_name: 3. A survey
text: of family firms. We define a dimension to be strictly necessary if the paper explicitly notes that

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the criterion is a necessary part of the set of firms defined as family firms; if the criterion is not

section_name: 3. A survey
text: met, the firm is considered non-family. An example is the definition of family firms employed in

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Fan and Leung (2020), who study publicly listed firms in Hong Kong and define family firms as

section_name: 3. A survey
text: those where a founder or a family member owns more than 10% of the shares. No other

section_name: 3. A survey
text: conditions are mentioned in their definition of family firms, and the ownership level exceeding

section_name: 3. A survey
text: the 10% threshold is a hard barrier. Firms in which the founding family’s ownership is less than

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 10% are not classified as family firms.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: [ Insert Figure 5 about here]

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 24 In the weakly necessary criteria, we include definitions where the criterion is part of an “or”

section_name: 3. A survey
text: condition, i.e., if this criterion is included then it is a family firm. An example of this is found in

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Ellul, Pagano, & Schivardi (2018) in which one of the definitions of a family firm is one where a

section_name: 3. A survey
text: founding family either has significant ownership or is involved in top management. In this case,

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Ownership is counted as a weakly necessary criterion – if it is met, the firm qualifies as a family

section_name: 3. A survey
text: firm, but even if it is not met, the firm may qualify as a family firm if the founding family

section_name: 3. A survey
text: descendants are represented in the firm’s top management (that is, the Management criterion is

section_name: 3. A survey
text: To both consider the strict and weakly necessary conditions, from Figure 5 we can see that

section_name: 3. A survey
text: ownership is clearly the most used dimension in both the groups. It appears 126 times as a

section_name: 3. A survey
text: weakly necessary condition in the definition of family firms, either by itself or in combination

section_name: 3. A survey
text: with other criteria. Of this, it appears 104 times as a strictly necessary condition – if the founding

section_name: 3. A survey
text: family fails to meet the ownership threshold, the firm is classified as a non-family firm. While

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Management and Board appear 71 and 51 times as weakly necessary criteria, we find that they

section_name: 3. A survey
text: are less likely to be strictly necessary criteria (34 and 16 times respectively), dramatically

section_name: 3. A survey
text: compared to the weakly necessary conditions. To put that in perspective, Ownership appears as a

section_name: 3. A survey
text: strictly necessary condition 83% (104/126) of the time it is included as a weakly necessary

section_name: 3. A survey
text: condition. Management and Board presence appear as strictly necessary conditions 48% (34/71)

section_name: 3. A survey
text: and 31% (16/51) of the time they are included as weakly necessary criteria.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: Embeddedness and Succession; however, not surprisingly are relatively rare, appearing 27

section_name: 3. A survey
text: and 14 times respectively as weakly necessary criteria in defining family firms in our survey.

section_name: 3. A survey
text: However, studies that do employ these two criteria in defining family firms, treat them more

section_name: 3. A survey
text: 25 often as a necessary criterion. Embeddedness and Succession appear as strictly necessary

section_name: 3. A survey
text: conditions 96% (26/27) and 93% (13/14) of the time they are included as weakly necessary

section_name: 3. A survey
text: conditions. Interestingly, we note that Embeddedness (26 times) is more commonly used as a

section_name: 3. A survey
text: strictly necessary criterion than the Board (16 times) is in our survey. By contrast, Succession as

section_name: 3. A survey
text: a strictly necessary criterion is least prominent in the literature on family business. This is

section_name: 3. A survey
text: interesting since as we discussed above; the succession motive is the key intent that separates

section_name: 3. A survey
text: entrepreneurial firms and founder-controlled firms from dynastic family firms.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: Donnelley (1964) attributed two dimensions – family embeddedness and succession - to a

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: family firm that distinguishes it from non-family firms. Since this publication of Donnelly in

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: 1964, a huge body of literature studying family firms. The prevalence, performance, and

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: innovation of family firms have been investigated with numerous empirical investigations, while

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: the key issue of appropriately distinguishing between family and non-family firms is kept

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: This survey identified several concerns for the lack of persuasive definition of family firm

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: from the extant studies. First, ownership was seldom used as a continuous variable – in almost all

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: of the papers we surveyed, ownership is defined as a binary variable based on ad hoc thresholds,

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: most commonly 5% of outstanding shares. It’s not surprising since ownership provides an easily

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: measurable quantitative measure that is not subject to ambiguity. However, the non-unified

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: threshold from 5% to 50% resulted in a dramatic variation in studying the pools of “family

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: firms”, hence raise questionable empirical findings.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: 26 Second, we argued that the two requirements from Donnelly (1964) lead to an unintended

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: outcome of such a facile definitional framework accepted by the literature is a straitjacket that

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: sometimes omits family firms because of insufficient founding family ownership and sometimes

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: includes firms as family controlled when the founder had little intent of passing on control to the

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: next generation and in fact, such a succession never took place. These two mis-categorizations

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: have the potential to introduce bias in several aspects of inferences one can draw from empirical

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: studies on family firms. Drawing inferences from empirical studies becomes difficult when

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: fundamental disagreements of identity are left unresolved.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: In this paper we have taken a first large sample examination of the status quo of family firm

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: definitions by decomposing them along five dimensions – Ownership, Management, Board,

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: Succession and Embeddedness. The first three are commonly encountered as part of the

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: definition employed by a great number of studies in literature, and the last two as dimensions

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: that are in the original Donnelley proposition, but not commonly employed in the vast number of

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: empirical studies over the last two decades. Our survey of 122 papers is a first step in taking

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: stock of the relative use of each of the five dimensions of family firm definition, and a useful

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: guide to understanding the current state of family firm classification. The precise accounting we

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: offer in our survey is helpful in drafting proposed definitions for future work that can overcome

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: the biases inherent in the current definitions employed in the literature.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: We highlight the fact that family firms are heterogeneous, despite the extant research using

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: a dichotomic variable to identify them. While we shy away from proposing an iron-clad

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: definition of family firms, we hope that the dimensions of embeddedness and succession which

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: were first identified by Donnelley are treated as integral parts of whatever definition the

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: 27 literature converges on. Emphasizing embeddedness corrects the other bias resulting from

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: excluding many firms that fail to meet ad hoc ownership thresholds, and the requirement of

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: succession intent or practice can appropriately eliminate all entrepreneur managed firms where

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: the founder has either no heirs or no bequest motive beyond wealth.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: We urgently call on researchers to move away from the data-driven definitions currently

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: used in the literature, which often lead to conflicting findings and obscure our understanding of

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: family firms. As a result, the current landscape of family firm research remains fragmented.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: Instead, we suggest that future research should focus on bridging theoretical definitions with

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: empirical categorizations, using multiple criteria for classification. This approach will enhance

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: our understanding of how the diverse group of firms labeled as "family firms" impact

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: governance and business outcomes.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: In conclusion, while we do not advocate for a rigid definition of family firms, we strongly

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: recommend incorporating the two key dimensions from Donnelley’s original 1964 framework.

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: We leave the task of developing robust categorizations for empirical research to future studies,

section_name: Discussion and Implications for Future Research
text: having laid the foundational groundwork in this survey.
